final_answer: 1. Check gateway logs for 502/504 patterns and response times (N1:0) to see if errors correlate with specific request paths or latency spikes. 2. Enable distributed tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) on the backend (N1:1) to measure latency of each downstream HTTP/TCP call and pinpoint slow services. 3. Monitor per‑service metrics (CPU, memory, request rate) after the release (N1:2) to detect any service that suddenly spikes. 4. Profile database queries (N1:3) and collect DB‑level metrics such as CPU usage, wait events, active sessions (N2:1). 5. Identify the top CPU‑consuming queries via pg_stat_statements or equivalent (N2:2) and examine their execution plans for missing indexes or inefficient joins (N2:3). 6. Check for lock contention or long‑running transactions (N2:4) that could be driving CPU usage. 7. Review recent code changes (N1:4) for added downstream calls or increased concurrency that may overload the DB or services. 8. Add circuit breakers around downstream services (N1:6) and implement request timeout and retry policies with back‑off (N1:7) to prevent cascading failures. 9. Enable or tune connection pooling (N2:6) so that the number of DB connections stays within CPU capacity. Follow this sequence to isolate the bottleneck—gateway, downstream latency, or DB query load—and apply the appropriate mitigation to stabilize the system.
